definition: The lowest price (wick or body) of the immediately preceding daily candle. Marks a sell-side liquidity pool where stop-loss orders from long positions accumulate. Serves as a key reference for directional bias, liquidity sweep targets, and support/resistance. A sweep followed by a close back inside the prior range signals potential bullish reversal; a sustained close below it purges the level.
identification_rules: Mark the lowest wick or body price of the completed prior daily candle as a horizontal line on the chart.
Label the level as sell-side liquidity when price is trading above it, indicating resting stop orders below.
A valid PDL sweep occurs when the current session's price trades below that horizontal line, triggering resting sell-side liquidity.
Confirm a bullish reversal sweep by observing a candle that closes back above the PDL level, forming a bullish reversal candle structure.
The level is considered purged (loses primary targeting function) once price trades through and sustains a close below it.
In a bearish New York reversal setup, look for price to take out the Previous Day Low during the New York AM session (08:30–12:00 EST) after London fails to reach a relevant higher time frame level.
When multiple prior daily lows are stacked or aligned, treat the cluster as a low-resistance liquidity zone rather than a single discrete level.

When daily bias is bearish, treat PDL as a downside draw on liquidity and a continuation target; confirm with higher time frame context such as engagement of a bearish fair value gap or a bearish daily candle closure.
To anticipate bearish continuation: if price fails to sweep PDL during consolidation after a downtrend, or if it displaces aggressively below PDL, expect further downside to the next PDL.

For bullish reversal setups: look for price to trade below PDL and then close back inside the prior day's range (sweep), and confirm with a lower time frame change in state of delivery (e.g., closure through the down-close candles that created the sweep).

After a sweep and reversal close, mark the 0.5 level of the wick (from candle body to wick low) on the lower time frame; bullish continuation requires the next session to open and hold above this level.
Use hourly or 30-minute charts during Asia/London, and 5-minute or 15-minute during New York to confirm change in state of delivery at PDL.
Pair PDL with nearby higher time frame PD arrays (e.g., daily fair value gap below it) to validate it as a meaningful target.
Look back three days to mark prior PDLs as reference liquidity; use repeated takings of successive PDLs to confirm active bearish trend.
In 4-hour or lower timeframes, continuously track the level as a key reference for the current day.
Observe whether price touches the level and closes below it: close below confirms displacement; touch only without close below is failure to displace.
If price pierces below PDL and closes back above (candle closes inside prior range), it is a liquidity sweep, forming high resistance liquidity.
If PDL is never touched or broken, it is a failure swing, forming low resistance liquidity.
If the prior day closed above previous day high, the draw on liquidity target for the current day shifts to PDL.
If price closes below PDL, the next target becomes the next prior day low.
When price approaches PDL, simultaneously observe correlated assets (e.g., ES vs NQ, EU vs GU) for SMT divergence.
trading_implication: PDL frames daily bias as a sell-side liquidity draw. In bearish contexts, it serves as a downside target; entries are taken on aligned lower timeframes after HTF confirmation, aiming for PDL or the next one after displacement. In bullish reversals, a sweep and close back inside the range, confirmed by a change in state of delivery and wick level respect, signals a long entry toward opposing liquidity (e.g., PDH, equal highs). Combined with fair value gaps and displacement analysis for refined timing. In bearish New York reversals, when London fails to reach a relevant HTF PD array, traders anticipate New York to sweep the PDL after a manipulation move higher; a confirmed change in state of delivery validates bearish continuation.
common_mistakes: Treating a London sweep of the PDL as a completed London reversal without verifying that a relevant higher time frame PD array was actually reached — if no HTF level was engaged, the setup may still be a New York reversal.
Assuming the PDL is the final target when multiple prior daily lows are stacked below it; price can continue through a cluster of previous day lows as low-resistance liquidity.
Entering a trade anticipating the PDL sweep before a confirmed change in the state of delivery has occurred on the intraday chart.
Treating a sweep of the PDL as a continuation signal rather than a potential reversal trigger — the close back inside the range is the critical confirmation step.
Entering long immediately on the PDL sweep without waiting for a close back above the level and confirmation of a bullish reversal candle.
Trading PDL sweeps or reversals without aligning to higher time frame bias (e.g., taking longs without a bullish HTF protected swing or ignoring consolidation range highs).
Expecting full expansion beyond PDL when the reversal candle has a large wick rather than a shallow one.
Assuming a protected swing will hold indefinitely once liquidity is taken — the protected swing itself can be swept before the true move.
Entering long before PDL sweep is confirmed by a close back inside the range and a change in state of delivery (e.g., entering during consolidation without sweep).
Taking a continuation entry toward PDL when the stop loss placement is too large to make the trade worthwhile.
Trusting a continuation entry without additional confirmation such as SMT on a correlated asset when the setup has not reached a valid prior level.
Trading the New York session for PDL when Asia has already taken the previous day low, leaving no target.
Ignoring that retracements and liquidity sweeps create up-close order blocks that must be respected before continuation lower.
Expecting an entry every day even when daily bias toward PDL is clear — a valid lower-time-frame setup is not always available.
Anticipating bullish expansion after a PDL sweep without confirming that price respects the upper half (0.5) of the reversal wick on the lower time frame.
Only looking at whether price touches PDL without checking if it closes below — closure is key to confirm displacement.
Ignoring trend context — in an uptrend, touching PDL should not be expected to lead to a large decline, otherwise it may signal a trend change.
Entering after a sweep without waiting for price to close back above the level, missing the high resistance liquidity confirmation.
Treating an untouched PDL (low resistance liquidity) as strong support to go long, rather than as a potential target for longs.
Entering solely on SMT divergence at PDL without waiting for structure alignment or displacement confirmation.
Treating SMT at PDL as the primary reversal reason rather than as confluence.

related_concepts.term: Change in the State of Delivery

merge_notes: All three projects are ICT school; no school conflict. The Foundation project included Chinese aliases and definitions; English definition used. Some identification rules from The Foundation were more detailed (e.g., SMT divergence, failure to displace) and were merged. No major disagreements found.
